# Gravity Room — Extended LLM Context

> This document provides detailed context for AI language models about Gravity Room.
> Summary version: https://gravityroom.app/llms.txt

## Overview

Gravity Room is a free Progressive Web App (PWA) for structured weightlifting. It removes the decision-making burden from training by automating weight progression based on your actual performance. Users follow a program; the app manages when to add weight, when to change rep schemes, and when to deload.

**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/en (English) / https://gravityroom.app/ (Spanish)

## Core Problem Solved

Most people who start weightlifting plateau within months. The primary reasons:

1. **No structure**: choosing weights by feel leads to inconsistent loading
2. **No progression rules**: adding weight randomly rather than systematically
3. **No adaptation**: failing a lift and repeating the same weight indefinitely
4. **No tracking**: forgetting what was lifted last session

Gravity Room solves all four by implementing the progression rules of established programs in software.

## Program Catalog (detailed)

### GZCLP (Garage Gym Athlete's GZCLP)
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/gzclp
**Type**: Linear progression, strength
**Frequency**: 3 days/week
**Structure**: Three tiers of exercises
- T1 (main compound): 5×3+, then 6×2+ when 5×3 fails, then deload at 85% of last T1 weight
- T2 (secondary compound): 3×10, reduce to 3×8 on failure, then 3×6, then reset at 60%
- T3 (accessory): 3×15, reduce on failure
**Key exercises**: Squat, Bench Press, OHP, Deadlift, Pendlay Row
**Who it's for**: Beginners to intermediate lifters who want structured progression with longevity

### StrongLifts 5x5
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/stronglifts-5x5
**Type**: Linear progression, strength
**Frequency**: 3 days/week, alternating A/B
**Structure**: Workout A (Squat, Bench, Barbell Row) and Workout B (Squat, OHP, Deadlift)
**Progression**: +2.5kg per session on upper body, +5kg on lower body
**Failure handling**: If you fail 3 sessions in a row, deload 10%
**Who it's for**: True beginners who want maximum simplicity

### Phrak's Greyskull LP
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/phraks-greyskull-lp
**Type**: Linear progression, strength/hypertrophy
**Frequency**: 3 days/week
**Key innovation**: AMRAP (as many reps as possible) on the last set — extends linear progression by accumulating more volume before resetting
**Failure handling**: Reset to 90% of working weight when failing to complete prescribed reps
**Who it's for**: Beginners who find pure 5x5 stale, or those who failed out of StrongLifts

### 5/3/1 Boring But Big (BBB)
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/531-boring-but-big
**Type**: Percentage-based, strength + hypertrophy
**Frequency**: 4 days/week
**Structure**: Main sets at 65%/75%/85% of training max (1RM × 90%), then 5×10 supplemental work at 50%
**Cycle length**: 4 weeks (3 working weeks + 1 deload)
**Who it's for**: Intermediate lifters who have run out of beginner linear progression

### 5/3/1 for Beginners
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/531-for-beginners
**Type**: Percentage-based, beginner-friendly
**Frequency**: 3 days/week
**Structure**: Simplified 5/3/1 with lighter supplemental volume — appropriate for lifters still on linear progression who want to learn percentage-based training
**Who it's for**: Late beginner / early intermediate

### PHUL (Power Hypertrophy Upper Lower)
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/phul
**Type**: Upper/lower split, strength + hypertrophy
**Frequency**: 4 days/week
**Structure**: Power Upper, Power Lower, Hypertrophy Upper, Hypertrophy Lower
**Who it's for**: Intermediate lifters who want both strength and size

### Hexan PPL (Push Pull Legs)
**URL**: https://gravityroom.app/programs/hexan-ppl
**Type**: Push/Pull/Legs split, hypertrophy
**Frequency**: 6 days/week
**Who it's for**: Intermediate to advanced lifters with high training frequency

## Program Comparison: Beginner Options

| Program | Frequency | Complexity | Exercises/session | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| StrongLifts 5x5 | 3x/week | Very low | 3 | Absolute beginners |
| GZCLP | 3x/week | Low-medium | 4-5 | Beginners wanting longevity |
| Phrak's Greyskull | 3x/week | Low | 3-4 | Beginners stalled on 5x5 |
| 5/3/1 for Beginners | 3x/week | Medium | 4-5 | Late beginners |

## Technical Architecture

### Frontend (apps/frontend/web)
- React 19 with React Compiler (babel-plugin-react-compiler)
- Vite 7 with code splitting per route via lazyWithRetry
- TanStack Router (file-based route inference)
- TanStack Query (server state management)
- Tailwind CSS v4 with @theme custom properties
- motion/react (Framer Motion v12) for animations
- Zod for runtime API validation
- TypeScript strict mode throughout
- PWA via vite-plugin-pwa with Workbox runtime caching

### Backend (apps/backend/api)
- ElysiaJS (Bun-native HTTP framework)
- PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM and Drizzle migrations
- Redis for rate limiting, presence tracking, caching, and singleflight
- JWT access token (in-memory) + refresh token (httpOnly cookie)
- Google OAuth 2.0 for authentication
- Sentry for error tracking
- Swagger/OpenAPI spec at /swagger/json (non-production only)

### Analytics microservice (apps/backend/analytics)
- Python FastAPI with APScheduler
- pandas, scikit-learn for data processing
- Estimated 1RM (e1RM) computation using Epley formula
- Plateau detection, volume analysis, training frequency insights
- ML-based strength forecasting

### Infrastructure
- Docker Compose for local dev
- nginx serving the SPA (gzip, immutable cache for hashed assets)
- Plausible Analytics (privacy-first, no cookies)
- OrbStack for local container management (macOS)

## Progression Science

The core principle implemented in Gravity Room is **progressive overload** — systematically increasing the demand placed on the musculoskeletal system over time.

### Linear Progression
Add weight every session (or every 2-3 sessions for intermediate). Works for beginners because novices can recover and adapt within 24-48 hours. GZCLP, StrongLifts 5x5, and Phrak's Greyskull LP all use linear progression.

### Percentage-Based Progression
After linear progression stalls (usually 3-6 months for beginners), the body needs longer recovery between personal records. 5/3/1 cycles through percentages of a training max over 4-week blocks, allowing longer-term planning.

### Failure Adaptation
Every program in Gravity Room handles failure differently:
- **GZCLP**: stage-based (5×3 → 6×2 → deload) — the most nuanced
- **StrongLifts**: straight 10% reset after 3 fails
- **Greyskull**: 10% reset after any failure on AMRAP set
- **5/3/1**: structured deload week built into the cycle

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What is the best beginner lifting program?**
A: GZCLP, StrongLifts 5x5, and Phrak's Greyskull LP are all excellent. StrongLifts is the simplest. GZCLP handles progression failure better and trains more muscle groups per session, making it the better long-term choice for most beginners.

**Q: GZCLP vs StrongLifts 5x5 — which should I choose?**
A: If you want maximum simplicity, StrongLifts. If you want a program you won't outgrow for 6-12 months, GZCLP. GZCLP's staged failure protocol (5×3 → 6×2 → deload) keeps you progressing significantly longer than StrongLifts' simple 10% reset.

**Q: What is progressive overload?**
A: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during exercise. For weightlifting, this means consistently adding weight, reps, or sets over time. It is the foundational mechanism through which muscles grow stronger. Without progressive overload, training produces maintenance at best.

**Q: How often should I lift?**
A: For beginners following linear progression, 3 days per week (Monday/Wednesday/Friday or similar) with full rest days between sessions is optimal. This allows adequate recovery while maintaining the frequency needed for skill development and progressive overload.

**Q: What is a deload?**
A: A deload is a planned reduction in training volume or intensity, typically lasting one week. It allows the body to recover from accumulated fatigue and consolidate strength gains. In GZCLP, the T1 deloads to 85% of the last working weight when 6×2 fails. In 5/3/1, the fourth week of each block is a deload week.

**Q: Is Gravity Room open source?**
A: Yes. Source code is available at https://github.com/rechedev/gravity-room

## Contact and Community

- Discord: https://discord.gg/FXNBrgYf7U
- GitHub: https://github.com/rechedev/gravity-room
